coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodots as a fluorescence sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and their multifunctional applications
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xue
Li, Tianze
Wu, Qiuhua
Yan, Xiao
Wu, Chenglin
Chen, Xia
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, carbon nanodots (CDs) were prepared via a green and convenient microwave assisted pyrolysis of ionic liquids (ILs) and ethylenediamine. It was found that the synthesized IL-based CDs (ILCDs) exhibited a superior selective sensitivity for Cr(VI). Photoluminescence (PL) properties of the ILCDs were applied to determine the concentration of Cr(VI), which showed a wide linear range and a low detection limit. We also received promising results by using the ILCDs for Cr(VI) detection in real samples. Moreover, the synthesized ILCDs can also work as a PL nanosensors to determine temperature and pH value in solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
Ionic liquids
Fluorescence sensor
Cr(VI) detection
Temperature detection
pH detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and composition of the ILCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–Vis and FT-IR spectra of the ILCDs.
(a) HR-TEM micrographs and magnification (inset) of the ILCDs. (b) DLS histogram of the ILCDs.
PL spectra of the ILCDs under different excitation wavelengths. Inset: normalized PL spectra of the ILCDs.

The maximal PL intensity of the ILCDs with different NaCl concentrations (blue solid line, excitation wavelength: 380nm) and continuous UV irradiation (red broken line, excitation wavelength: 365nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

PL intensity of the ILCDs solutions containing different cations, anions and H2O2 (excitation wavelength: 380nm; F0 and F are the maximal PL intensity of the ILCDs in the absence and presence of additives, respectively).

(a) PL spectra of the ILCDs with different concentrations of Cr(VI) (0–150.0μM). (b) The relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and Cr(VI) concentration. Inset in (b): linear correlation plot in the Cr(VI) concentration ranging from 0 to 10.0μM. (excitation wavelength: 380nm; F0 and F are the maximal PL intensity of the ILCDs at 458nm in the absence and presence of Cr(VI), respectively).
(a) PL spectra of the ILCDs with different pH values (from 1.0 to 13.0); (b) pH dependence of the normalized PL intensity of the ILCDs. (excitation wavelength: 380nm).

(a) PL spectra of the ILCDs solution during continuous heating-up from 20.0 to 90.0°C; (b) Temperature dependence of the normalized PL intensity of the ILCDs; (c) The maximal PL intensity of the ILCDs during repeating heating-cooling cycles at 90.0 and 20.0°C.
Schematic representation of the synthesis, the structure and the sensing applications of the ILCDs.
Comparison of the performance of the presented method with some other published analytical techniques for Cr(VI) detection.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots as a fluorescence sensor for rapid and sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and their multifunctional applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Province Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry, Liaoning University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Key Laboratory of Membrane Materials and Membrane Separation, Guangzhou Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Guangzhou 511458, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Key Laboratory of Membrane Materials and Membrane Separation, Guangzhou Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Institute of Advanced Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical and Chemical Engineering, Taizhou University, Taizhou 317000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical and Chemical Engineering, Taizhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, carbon nanodots (CDs) were prepared via a green and convenient microwave assisted pyrolysis of ionic liquids (ILs) and ethylenediamine. It was found that the synthesized IL-based CDs (ILCDs) exhibited a superior selective sensitivity for Cr(VI). Photoluminescence (PL) properties of the ILCDs were applied to determine the concentration of Cr(VI), which showed a wide linear range and a low detection limit. We also received promising results by using the ILCDs for Cr(VI) detection in real samples. Moreover, the synthesized ILCDs can also work as a PL nanosensors to determine temperature and pH value in solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent years, carbon nanodots (CDs) have attracted a lot of attention because of their fascinating optical, chemical and biological properties [1–3]. Compared with the other fluorescent nanomaterials, such as semiconductor nanodots (QDs) with heavy metals as the essential composition to accomplish their high performance PL, the biocompatibility of CDs is better, and the preparation of CDs is more convenient and green.

CDs can be prepared through chemical reactions using universal carbon sources, from small molecular precursors, carbon materials and natural bioorganic precursors to even food waste [1–3]. Moreover, through doping with various heteroatoms, like N-doping, S-doping and P-doping, CDs can obtain novel surface state and unusual PL features, including pH or thermal response and high quantum yield (Ф). Therefore, we can choose appropriate carbon source with definite heteroatoms to prepare CDs with superior properties.

Ionic liquids (ILs) are kinds of liquid salt, which have attracted tremendous attention because of their fascinating and valuable physicochemical features, such as high ion conductivity, chemical stability, non-flammability, and near absence of vapor pressure [4–6]. Among various structures of ILs, imidazolium-type ILs possess aromatic structure and superior nitrogen content, which is admirably suited as the carbon source to prepare CDs. For example, Hao et al. used citric acid and 1-aminopropyl-3-methy-imidazolium bromide ([APMIm+][Br-]) ILs to prepare CDs [7], which can transfer between water and oil phase spontaneously through facilely anion exchange. MacFarlane et al. applied N-methylethanolammonium thioglycolate ([MEAm+][HSCH2COO-]) as a single carbon source to prepare CDs [8], and such CDs can act as a sensor for ultrasensitive pesticide detection.

Water pollution has been a major global problem, which has attracted the attention of both the governments and scientific research institutions. Toxic heavy metal ions are one of the main pollution sources to water. Diversified chemosensors towards various heavy metal ions have been constructed and applied to monitor the water quality [9,10], and CDs are just one type of them [3,11]. Compared with the other chemosensors, CDs have advantages in their abundant synthetic sources, simple synthetic method, as well as environment-friendly performance.

The poisonous and carcinogenic features of Cr(VI) (chromium) have been known since at least the late 19th century. In ore dressing, leather tanning, metal plating and printing and dying industry, amounts of Cr compounds are discharged without further processing, which can eventually cause severe environmental problems [12]. The concentration of Cr(VI) in potable water is severely controlled to micromolar level by many countries. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has announced that 0.1mgL−1 Cr in potable water might result in healthy risk [13]. The World Health Organization declared that 50μgL−1 Cr(VI) is ought to be careful, due to their high risk of carcinogenicity and genotoxicity [14]. In view of the high risk of Cr in solution, simple and sensitive methods towards the Cr level in actual samples are very significant, which has been reported by several research groups [15–28]. However, the methods aiming at Cr(VI) are actually rare. Liu and co-workers utilized CDs capped with polyethyleneimine (CD-PEI) as a highly sensitive and selective sensor for Cr(VI) assay [28].

Herein, we report a new one-step method to prepare fluorescent CDs through microwave assisted pyrolysis of ILs (1-vinyl-3-aminopropyl imidazolium, [PAVIm+][Br-]) and ethylenediamine mixture. In our synthesis system, ILs acted as carbon source, and ethylenediamine molecules served as dual roles, including N-doping and surface-passivizing agent. The synthesized CDs (ILCDs) possess excellent PL property, which can be applied to determine the concentration of Cr(VI). In addition, it is found that the ILCDs can also serve as multifunctional fluorescence nanosensors to realize the quantitative determination of temperature and pH value. The preparation, the structure, and the sensing applications of the ILCDs are presented in Scheme 1.

[PAVIm+][Br-] was synthesized using a literature procedure [29]. Ethylenediamine were ordered from Bodi Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China) and used without further purification. All the other reagents were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were of analytical grade, and therefore we used them directly without further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout all experiments.

The ILCDs were synthesized through microwave irradiation using a domestic microwave oven. First of all, 0.1g [PAVIm+][Br-] and 0.1mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 5.0mL ultrapure water. The baker with the mixed solution was placed in the microwave oven, and 700W power was settled. After incubating for 300s, yellow solid ILCDs were obtained, and 10.0mL ultrapure water was added to dissolve the crude ILCDs. The insoluble residue was gotten rid of through centrifugation at 12,000 revolutions per minute (rpm) for 20min. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed against ultrapure water for 48h to remove the residual [PAVIm+][Br-] and ethylenediamine (dialysis membrane molecular weight cut off =100–500Da, Shanghai Baoman Biological technology Co., Ltd). Before further characterization and use, the obtained ILCDs samples were preserved in the refrigerator at 4°C.

The PL quantum yield (Ф) of the ILCDs was calculated according to a comparative method [30]. Quinine sulfate with a standard PL quantum yield (54%), the maximum emission wavelength of which was close to that of the ILCDs, was chosen as the reference to determine the PL quantum yield of the obtained ILCDs. First of all, standard curve of quinine sulfate was plotted. A series of quinine sulfate solutions in 0.1MH2SO4 were prepared with different absorbance values, which was followed at the excitation wavelength of the maximum PL emission of quinine sulfate (340nm). In order to minimize re-absorption effects, the absorbance values should never exceed 0.1, according to the size of fluorescence cuvette we used (10mm). Meanwhile, the PL emission spectra were recorded (excitation wavelength: 340nm) and processed through integration. The integral area of these integrated PL emission spectra were calculated in the wavelength range from 350 to 600nm. A graph was plotted using the integral area values against the absorbance values, and a trend line was added for the curve with intercept at zero to obtain the gradient as GR. Using a similar method, GT owing to the pending CDs can be achieved. The PL quantum yield (Ф) of the ILCDs was calculated according to the following equation:
Φ
T
=
Φ
R
(
G
T
G
R
)
(
η
T
2
η
R
2
)
Where Φ R is the known PL quantum yield of quinine sulfate (54%), GT and GR are the gradients from the plot of the integral area vs absorbance at the excitation wavelength of CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

First of all, the ILCDs solution was prepared with the absorbance value of 0.05 (λab=380nm, simultaneously as the excitation wavelength of PL). On this basis, Cr(VI) assay was performed. Cr(VI) solutions (in phosphate buffer saline) with a calculated concentration (0, 0.05, 0.3, 1.0, 1.5, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 15.0, 20.0, 30.0, 50.0, 70.0, 90.0, 110.0, 130.0, 150.0μM) were prepared. 0.8mL Cr(VI) solution was added into 3.2mL ILCDs solution and mixed to equilibrium. After the stabilization of PL emission, the PL spectra were recorded (λex=380nm). A standard curve was plotted using the maximal fluorescence intensity against the Cr(VI) concentration. According to the standard curve, quantitative detection of Cr(VI) can be realized.

In order to test the performance of the method in practical samples, the local running water was selected as the background solvent. The analysis process was similar to the above-mentioned method, expect that the pending solution was prepared through mixing 0.4mL running water and 0.4mL Cr(VI) (in phosphate buffer saline) with a calculated concentration (0, 4.0, 8.0, 12.0, 16.0 and 20.0μM).

UV–Vis data were collected on a Purkinje General TU-1900 (China). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrometer with KBr pellets. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was applied to observe the morphology of the ILCDs, which was carried out on a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). The HR-TEM samples were prepared through dropping the ILCD solution on a copper grid, which was covered with a carbon film. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was tested to determine the grain diameter and size distribution of the ILCDs, which was operated on a Malvern Nano ZS instrument. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements were recorded by RF-5301PC (SHIMADZU, Japan). Both the excitation and emission slits were set as 5 and 5nm, respectively. The finally present fluorescence spectra were processed through normalized method, which were obtained through dividing each PL intensity of the PL spectra by the maximum value of their own. The fluorescence lifetime was measured using Fluorolog (Horiba Jobin Yvon) with a TCSPC system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and composition of the ILCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the previously reported CDs, the ILCDs absorb mainly in the UV region, which extends until the near-visible range ( Fig. 1a) [1]. Two main absorption bands appear in the spectrum, including the main band at 225nm assigned to the π-π* (C=C) transitions and the weak peak at 289nm (Fig. 1a inset) due to the n-π*(C=O) transitions [31]. The FT-IR results can reveal the characteristic functional groups on the ILCDs. As seen from Fig. 1b, the FT-IR spectrum exhibits the characteristic absorption bands, including stretching vibration of O-H or N-H ( 3435cm−1), stretching vibrations of C-H ( 2917 and 2850cm−1), stretching vibration of C˭C (1635cm−1), bending vibration of C-H (1385cm−1), and stretching vibration of C-O (1187 and 1042cm−1), which demonstrates that there might exist hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino in the ILCDs.

The HR-TEM test can present a direct observation about the morphology and size of the ILCDs. The HR-TEM micrograph in Fig. 2a reveals that the ILCDs are nearly spherical with a grain diameter of 5.5±1.9nm (20 particles were randomly chosen and measured). The magnified HR-TEM micrograph (Fig. 2a inset) shows regions of both graphitic with lattice fringes and amorphous carbon. The DLS results can further act as the secondary proof for the grain diameter of the ILCDs, as well as its good distribution, which is in good agreement with the results from the HR-TEM.

PL behavior is one of our most concern features of CDs, no matter from the fundamental research or their practical application. As can be seen from Fig. 3, a series of incremental excitation lights were applied to irradiate, which resulted in a set of various PL spectra of the ILCDs. Different from small fluorescence molecule, CDs show a characteristic excitation wavelength dependence of PL behavior [1]. The normalized PL intensity of the ILCDs are listed in Fig. 3 inset. We can see that the position of the emission bands of the ILCDs indeed slowly red-shifts with the increment of excitation wavelength. Meanwhile, for each kind of CDs, there usually exist an optimum excitation wavelength corresponding to a maximal PL intensity. As for our ILCDs, the optimum excitation wavelength is 380nm. The as-prepared ILCDs reveal a notable quantum yield of ca. 20.1%.

PL stability is very important for fluorescence materials. In some harsh conditions, such as high ionic strength or long-time irradiation, fluorescence bleaching might happen to some fluorescence materials, which affects their PL performance and restricts their large-scale applications. To our delight, the ILCDs exhibit a high stability of the photoemission towards ionic strength and UV irradiation. As presented in Fig. 4, the normalized maximal PL intensity of the ILCDs remains constant upon adding NaCl up to 1.0M. Interestingly, some resultant CDs can inherit crucial functional groups from the original carbon sources [32,33]. In our system, the ILCDs that prepared from [PAVIm+][Br-] possess many peripheral bulky imidazolium cations that originated from [PAVIm+][Br-]. The positive charges around the ILCDs are delocalized, which will help to reduce the binding of small counter ions. At the same time, the bulky imidazolium cations may also create a steric stabilization effect, accounting for the observed high stability against ionic strength [7]. In addition, after UV irradiating for 6h, the normalized maximal PL intensity of the ILCDs is still higher than 0.8, reflecting their good anti-photobleaching behavior.

Lots of literatures have reported that CDs can act sensor for different chemicals, especially many metal ions [3]. Therefore, we checked the PL variation of the ILCDs with various cations (Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, NH4 +, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sr2+, and Zn2+), anions (Br-, Cl-, ClO3 -, CO3 2-, F-, I-, NO2 -, NO3 -, PO4 3-, SCN-, SO3 2-, SO4 2-, SiO3 2-, S2O3 2-, WO4 2-, [Fe(CN)6]3-, [Fe(CN)6]4-, and MnO4 -) and H2O2 ( Fig. 5). The experimental results demonstrated that the synthesized ILCDs displayed obvious sensitivity for Cr(VI), Mn(VII) and [Fe(CN)6]3-, and the sensitivity for Cr(VI) was the best.

In order to investigate the sensitivity and detection limit of the ILCDs based Cr(VI) sensor, a series of concentrations of Cr(VI) (0–150.0μM) were prepared and mixed with the ILCDs. In Fig. 6a, we can see that, the PL intensity of the ILCDs gradually decreases with the increase of Cr(VI) concentration. The PL quenching ratio (F0-F)/F0 of the ILCDs is related with Cr(VI) concentration (Fig. 6b). Interestingly, there exists a linear correlation in the Cr(VI) concentration range of 0.05–10.0μM (Fig. 6b inset), which indicates that we can perform quantitative determination of Cr(VI) using the ILCDs. The linear equation is fitting as (F0-F)/F0 =0.0222×C Cr (μM)+0.0047 (R2=0.994). The detection limit is calculated to be 0.015μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Moreover, the detection limit of PL for Cr(VI) is lower than the other reported test methods [15–25], which demonstrates that such Cr(VI) sensor based on the ILCDs is more sensitive ( Table 1). Meanwhile, the guideline value recommended by World Health Organization (WHO) is 50μgL−1 (ca. 1μM) in potable water [14], which just lies in the detection range of our ILCDs-based Cr(VI) detection system.

In order to investigate the practicability of the present method, local running water was chosen as the background interfere to perform Cr(VI) detection. The analysis results are exhibited in Table 2. Random concentrations of Cr(VI) were designed and added into ILCDs solution. The Cr(VI) concentration in local running water was determined to be 0M, and therefore Cr(VI) in solution came only from manual addition. Compared with the initial addition of Cr(VI), the final recoveries were determined between 98.0% and 103.1%, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were in the range of 1.40–2.31%. These results revealed that the ILCDs based fluorescent sensor could be applied under certain environmental interfere.

The PL quenching mechanism of CDs against metal ions is still a matter of current debate and requires further clarification. However, there are several explanations for this, such as fluorescence inner filter effect (IFE) or ions binding effect. In order to figure out whether ions binding effect account for the sensing mechanism, the variation of the FT-IR spectra and the fluorescence lifetime of the ILCDs were monitored before and after adding various ions, the results are shown in the supporting information. In Fig. S1, we can see that, there was no obvious variation in the FT-IR spectra of the ILCDs before and after adding various ions, indicating the chemical bonds or functional groups around the ILCDs did not change during the sensing process. The fluorescence lifetime spectra of the ILCDs before and after adding various ions were shown in Fig. S2. The fluorescence decay curves of all samples can be fitted by a single-exponential formula with a similar lifetime of ca. 5ns, which indicates no obvious electron transfer appearing in the sensing system. These two results can effectively confirm the mode of reaction/binding is not dominant in the sensing process of the ILCDs against Cr(VI).

Inner filter effect (IFE) usually requires a spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber and the excitation band and/or emission band of the fluorophore [34]. In our system, we can see that, the UV–Vis spectrum of Cr(VI) in Fig. 7 reveals two strong absorption bands located at approximately 258 and 351nm, and a weak band at around 450nm. Meanwhile, using the optimal excitation wavelength (λex=380nm) to irritate the ILCDs, the emission band of the ILCDs is centered at 458nm (Fig. 3), which results in an obvious overlap between the absorption bands of Cr(VI) and the emission bands, as well as the exciting light of the ILCDs. Consequently, Cr(VI) can shield both the excitation and the emission light of the ILCDs. Furthermore, the higher the Cr(VI) concentration is, the more excitation/emission light they can absorb, resulting in the higher fluorescence quenching efficiency of the ILCDs.

In the above-mentioned research, the synthesized ILCDs show good PL stability towards ionic strength. However, interestingly, the ILCDs was found to be sensitive to environmental pH. As shown in Fig. 8, during the pH values ranging from 6.0 to 13.0, a good linear relationship exists: NI=−0.0602×pH+1.3802 with R2=0.994 (NI: Normalized Intensity). According to the FT-IR results in Fig. 1b, there are abundant carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of CDs. The protonation and deprotonation of these groups can account for pH response of the ILCDs [35]. The pH dependent property offers a great chance for applying the ILCDs as pH-monitoring devices.

The synthesized ILCDs also display a temperature response property. In Fig. 9a, during the temperature range from 20.0 to 90.0°C, with the increase of temperature, the PL intensity of the ILCDs decreases gradually. A fine linear region (NI =−0.0047×T+1.0842, R2=0.991) was observed between temperature and the maximal PL intensity (Fig. 9b). Moreover, during several heating-cooling cycles at 90.0 and 20.0°C, the PL intensity of the ILCDs remains stable at corresponding temperatures (Fig. 9c). Heating can increase the non-radiative channels of the surface (trap/defect) states on the ILCDs, which results in their quantitative PL decline [36,37].

In summary, we have demonstrated a facile and cheap one-step microwave treatment method to prepare the ILCDs, using [PAVIm+][Br-] ILs and ethylenediamine as the raw materials. The synthesized ILCDs behave stable in aqueous solutions, which can endure high ionic strength and long-time irradiation. It is a remarkable fact that the synthesized ILCDs can be sensitive to Cr(VI), through monitoring the quenching effect of fluorescence, which can be applied in real samples and match with the WHO criterion of Cr(VI) detection in drinking water. Based on the ILCDs, a multifunctional sensing platform can also be established for pH and temperature.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (51403093, 51373073, 51502045 and 51403149) and Shenzhen Basic Research Program (SY494231001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2016.12.037.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.
